title: W8 C Suggested Exercises
body:
Suggested Exercises for Week 8
                                    Juan Carlos Carbajal

                               ECON 3202 — Term 1, 2026
                                        UNSW Sydney


Problem 1. Let f : I → R be a function defined on an interval I ⊆ R.
 (a) Show that if f is concave and c ≥ 0, then c f is a concave function.
 (b) Show that if f is concave and c ≤ 0, then c f is a convex function.
  (c) Are the statements in parts (a) and (b) still true if f is instead defined on a
      convex set S ⊆ R n ?


Problem 2. In class we have stated that a differentiable function f : I → R is concave
if and only if its derivative D f is a weakly decreasing function. We omitted the proof
of this result because it is a bit complex. However, the intuition behind it is not too
complicated. Take x < y < z on the interval I. If f is concave, then the slope of the
ray connecting the point ( x, f ( x )) and the point (y, f (y)) is steeper than the slope of
the ray connecting the point (y, f (y)) and the point (z, f (z)).1
  The next proposition says that a slightly stronger property not only is necessary
but also sufficient for f to be concave. Prove it.

   Proposition 1

   Let I ⊆ R be an interval and f : I → R. The following are equivalent:

     (a) f is concave.

     (b) For all x, y, z ∈ I such that x < y < z, one has
                               f (y) − f ( x )   f (z) − f ( x )   f (z) − f (y)
                                               ≥                 ≥               .
                                   y−x               z−x               z−y




 1 Here a picture will help.




                                                    1
 Problem 3. In class we mentioned a few examples of concave and convex functions.
Verify that these examples are correct.

 (a) The absolute value | x | is a convex function with domain I = R.
 (b) The natural logarithm ln( x ) is a concave function with domain I = R ++ .
  (c) The exponential exp( x ) is a convex function with domain I = R.
 (d) The function h( x ) = xr defined on I = R + is concave for 0 < r < 1 and convex
     for r > 1.
  (e) The density function of the normal distribution
                                                  󰀋   2󰀌
                                 φ( x ) = √1 exp − x2
                                                       2π

      is neither convex nor concave.

Hint: Part (a) was verified in class using the definition of convex functions. For parts (b) to
(d), notice these functions are all twice differentiable, and thus you can evaluate the sign of
the second derivative. Part (e) is complicated, thus a picture will suffice.


Problem 4. Consider the function g( x ) = α e βx defined on R. Determine whether this
function is concave or convex. Will your answer depend on the value the parameters
α and β take?


Problem 5. Let u : R n+ → R be a continuous function —think of it as a utility function
for a consumer. Fix a given x̂ ∈ R n+ —think of this as a bundle of goods that the
consumer enjoys. Now define NWT( x̂) as the set of bundles in R n+ that provide the
consumer either the same utility or more utility than x̂ (thus, NWT( x̂) can be called
the set of bundles that are no-worse-than x̂):

                            NWT( x̂) = { x ∈ R n+ : u( x) ≥ u( x̂)}.

 (a) Show that NWT( x̂) is non-empty.
 (b) Show that NWT( x̂) is closed. This is a bit hard, so you may want to leave it. It helps to
      remember that u is continuous, and that a set S is closed if and only if the limit of any convergent
      sequence of elements belonging to S also belongs to S.

  (c) Show that if u is concave, then NWT( x̂) is a convex set.


Problem 6. Let f : R n → R. Show that if f is a linear function, then it is both concave
and convex. Does this continue to hold if f is an affine function?




                                                   2
